title: Increase Machine Disk Size has no effect
body:
### Issue Description

When I try to increase the disk size of my podman machine it shows it correctly in the interface when the machine is stopped but once the machine starts it does not reflect the new size. 
this also happens when doing it via the CLI. 
I'm running a Mac Mini M4. 

I added my remarks in #20564 a month ago to no effect. 

### Steps to reproduce the issue

Steps to reproduce the issue
1. Create a machine
2. Increase the disk size of the machine
3. Check the size of the machine


### Describe the results you received

The machine shows still the old size

### Describe the results you expected

The machine should show and use the new disk size.

comments.body: Please provide a proper reproducer with exact commands used.

The Manpage for `podman machine set` says for the `--disk-size` option: "Only supported for QEMU machines" - so it seems expected that it does not work on Mac. The bug would be that we're allowing this without an error.

@baude Is this a possible feature request, or do the Mac virtualization providers not allow growing images?

A reviewer has determined we need more information to understand the reported issue.  A comment on what is missing should be provided. Be certain you:

* provide an exact reproducer where possible
* verify you have provided all relevant information - minimum is `podman info`
* answer any follow up questions

If no response to the `needs-info` is provided in 30 days, this issue may be closed by our stale bot.

For more information on reporting issues on this repository, consult our [issue guide](ISSUE.md).

@mheon you caught me in the middle of triaging this myself.   I agree the reporter needs to fill in some details, but taking a guess here on what the reporter means.  I think the following sort of points it out.

1. Create a machine with a disk (say 20G)
```
bbaude@bbaude-mac ~ % podman machine init --disk-size 20
```
2. Start the machine
3. Check the actual disk size in the host fs
```
bbaude@bbaude-mac ~ % ls -lh ~/.local/share/containers/podman/machine/applehv/podman-machine-default-arm64.raw
-rw-r--r--@ 1 bbaude  staff    20G Apr 16 15:05 /Users/bbaude/.local/share/containers/podman/machine/applehv/podman-machine-default-arm64.raw
```
4. Check what the guest sees
```
bbaude@bbaude-mac ~ % podman machine ssh df -h
Filesystem                            Size  Used Avail Use% Mounted on
/dev/vda4                              20G  2.5G   17G  13% /sysroot
```
5. Stop the machine
6. Increase the disk size
```
bbaude@bbaude-mac ~ % podman machine set --disk-size 50
```
7. Check what the host sees
```
bbaude@bbaude-mac ~ % !ls
ls -lh ~/.local/share/containers/podman/machine/applehv/podman-machine-default-arm64.raw
-rw-r--r--@ 1 bbaude  staff    50G Apr 16 15:08 /Users/bbaude/.local/share/containers/podman/machine/applehv/podman-machine-default-arm64.raw
```
8. Start the machine
9. Check what the guest sees
```
Machine "podman-machine-default" started successfully
bbaude@bbaude-mac ~ % podman machine ssh df -h
Filesystem                            Size  Used Avail Use% Mounted on
/dev/vda4                              20G  2.5G   17G  13% /sysroot
```
Note the filesystem has not increased though the raw disk has.

If you `podman machine ssh`, you can see that the disk is in fact 50G.  And there are basically 30G of free space at the end of the disk.  The free space would need to be added to the /dev/vda4 (root) and then of course `xfs_growfs /dev/vda4`.

```
core@localhost:~$ sudo fdisk -l
GPT PMBR size mismatch (41943039 != 104857599) will be corrected by write.
The backup GPT table is not on the end of the device.
Disk /dev/vda: 50 GiB, 53687091200 bytes, 104857600 sectors
Units: sectors of 1 * 512 = 512 bytes
Sector size (logical/physical): 512 bytes / 512 bytes
I/O size (minimum/optimal): 512 bytes / 512 bytes
Disklabel type: gpt
Disk identifier: 5320C3BC-150B-4A65-BE5B-80E395532F29

Device       Start      End  Sectors  Size Type
/dev/vda1     2048     4095     2048    1M Linux reserved
/dev/vda2     4096   264191   260096  127M EFI System
/dev/vda3   264192  1050623   786432  384M Linux filesystem
/dev/vda4  1050624 41943006 40892383 19.5G Linux filesystem
```

I'm not sure how we automate this short of having a systemd service that checks this on boot and does this but I also think reboots to get the partition table in the kernel correctly (at least that used to be the case) ?

Disclaimer: USE AT YOUR OWN RISK

@milafrerichs one thing you can do manually for now is:
```
core@localhost:~$ sudo growpart /dev/vda 4
CHANGED: partition=4 start=1050624 old: size=40892383 end=41943006 new: size=103806943 end=104857566
core@localhost:~$ sudo mount -o remount,rw /sysroot
core@localhost:~$ sudo xfs_growfs -d /sysroot/
meta-data=/dev/vda4              isize=512    agcount=15, agsize=360320 blks
         =                       sectsz=512   attr=2, projid32bit=1
         =                       crc=1        finobt=1, sparse=1, rmapbt=1
         =                       reflink=1    bigtime=1 inobtcount=1 nrext64=1
data     =                       bsize=4096   blocks=5111547, imaxpct=25
         =                       sunit=0      swidth=0 blks
naming   =version 2              bsize=4096   ascii-ci=0, ftype=1
log      =internal log           bsize=4096   blocks=102600, version=2
         =                       sectsz=512   sunit=0 blks, lazy-count=1
realtime =none                   extsz=4096   blocks=0, rtextents=0
data blocks changed from 5111547 to 12975867
```

You might have to reboot to get /sysroot back to `ro`.
@milafrerichs also, can you confirm I am interpreting your issue correctly?
For context from a conversation between @baude and I, it seems like the line in the manpages about this being unsupported is in error; we do expect it to work.

the immediate fix is to update the documentation that the command simply increases the disk size but does not extend to actually going into the OS and reworking the partition tables and filesystem.  This is apparently true for all providers.  I'll assign this to myself for now and the upcoming bug week.

@baude and @mheon I‘m currently travelling and cannot give you more details at this point. But I can confirm that is what I’m trying. If I increase the disk-size of the machine it does not have an effect on the host and I run into not enough space errors when trying to pull images. 
I also tried using the Mac App. When the machine is stopped it shows the correct size but when the machine is started it falls back to the old one.

> the immediate fix is to update the documentation that the command simply increases the disk size but does not extend to actually going into the OS and reworking the partition tables and filesystem. This is apparently true for all providers. I'll assign this to myself for now and the upcoming bug week.

That would be news to me, when this feature was added originally I remember exactly that I asked if the OS resizes the disk automatically on the next boot and the answer was yes (https://github.com/containers/podman/pull/13953#discussion_r855434201).

So if our coreos images no longer resize the disk that it must have been broken at some point after that.

@baude  @mheon any update on this? What do I need to provide to you? 
I used the Mac app to do the disk update but when I run the `sudo fdisk -l` I see that the disk size is actually what it should be. And also if I stop the machine I can see it in the Mac app as well.

![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/097005dc-5b1e-4a66-a027-aa6a15810579)

![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/6eb54108-275b-43a9-9327-85b48db8f65f)

```
root@localhost:~# sudo fdisk -l
GPT PMBR size mismatch (27262975 != 52428799) will be corrected by write.
The backup GPT table is not on the end of the device.
Disk /dev/vda: 25 GiB, 26843545600 bytes, 52428800 sectors
Units: sectors of 1 * 512 = 512 bytes
Sector size (logical/physical): 512 bytes / 512 bytes
I/O size (minimum/optimal): 512 bytes / 512 bytes
Disklabel type: gpt
Disk identifier: 0C3DD09F-9409-4429-BEA8-E1A89A4AD2BA

Device       Start      End  Sectors  Size Type
/dev/vda1     2048     4095     2048    1M Linux reserved
/dev/vda2     4096   264191   260096  127M EFI System
/dev/vda3   264192  1050623   786432  384M Linux filesystem
/dev/vda4  1050624 27262942 26212319 12.5G Linux filesystem
```

I'm not sure I want to use the commands you gave me but if there is no fix coming soon I might need to.
